revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Shinto shrine]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24644718927|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Awayamanoueno Shrine]][[ja:阿波山上神社]] {{Expand Japanese|阿波山上神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Awayamanoueno Shrine | native_name = 阿波山上神社 | image = Awayamanoueno Shrine (Shirosato, Ibaraki, Japan) 03.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Awayamanoueno Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.519491|140.362817}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|ca|Sukunabikona|en|Sukunabikona|fr|Sukunabikona|it|Sukuna-hikona|ja|スクナビコナ|ko|스쿠나비코나|mni|ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ|pl|Sukunabikona|pt|Sukunahikona|simple|Sukunabikona|sv|Suku-Na-Bikona|zh|少彥名|qid=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}} }} {{nihongo|'''Awayamanoueno Shrine'''|阿波山上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Hitachi Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Q135160342|ltq=Q135160342|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}} * {{ill|Q135026601|ltq=Q135026601|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11481683|1=List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province}} ** Series ordinal: 17 ** P155: {{ill|Q11414032|ltq=Q11414032|qid=Q11414032|1=Yoshida Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine|lt=Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine|en|Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine|ja|酒列磯前神社|simple|Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine|zh|酒列磯前神社|qid=Q11644289|1=Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q1203068|ltq=Q1203068|qid=Q1203068|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki}} * {{ill|Hitachi Province|lt=Hitachi Province|cs|Provincie Hitači|de|Provinz Hitachi|en|Hitachi Province|es|Provincia de Hitachi|fa|ولایت هیتاچی|fr|Province de Hitachi|gl|Provincia de Hitachi|hak|Hitachi-koet|id|Provinsi Hitachi|it|Hitachi (provincia)|ja|常陸国|ko|히타치국|lt|Hitačio žemė|nl|Hitachi (provincie)|pt|Província de Hitachi|ru|Хитати (провинция)|simple|Hitachi Province|sk|Hitači (provincia)|sr|Провинција Хитачи|tr|Hitachi (vilayet)|uk|Провінція Хітаті|zh_min_nan|Hitati-kok|zh_yue|常陸國|zh|常陸國|qid=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}} * {{ill|Q1062968|ltq=Q1062968|qid=Q1062968|1=Naka District, Ibaraki}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.519491|140.362817}} == image (P18) == * Awayamanoueno Shrine (Shirosato, Ibaraki, Japan) 03.jpg == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Q3275687|ltq=Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 181770 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Gō-sha|lt=Gō-sha|ja|郷社|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: form (P1454) == * {{ill|Q7311343|ltq=Q7311343|qid=Q7311343|1=religious corporation}} == country (P17) == * ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * あわやまのうえのじんじゃ * あわやまのえのじんじゃ == postal code (P281) == * 311-4344https://www.houjin-bangou.nta.go.jp/download/zenken/index.html == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9050005000569 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県東茨城郡城里町阿波山664-1', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:阿波山上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11657523}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Awayamanoue Shrine |Image=[[File:Awayamanoueno Shrine (Shirosato, Ibaraki, Japan) 03.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashiibaraki District|Q1154667|qid=Q1154667|1=Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki}}{{ill|lt=Shirosato Town|Q1203068|qid=Q1203068|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki}} Awayama 664-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system#Other shrines|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |Founded = First year of {{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|qid=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=701|Q23496|qid=Q23496|1=701}}) |Main hall = |Other names = Furuki Myōjin |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = April 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Awaya
Manoue Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Awayamanoue Shrine''' (あわやまのうえのじんじゃ; also read as あわやまのえのじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located on Mount Awayama in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Higashiibaraki District|Q1154667|qid=Q1154667|1=Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Shirosato Town|Q1203068|qid=Q1203068|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki}}. It is also known by the alternative name “Furuki Myōjin.” It is identified with the “Awayamanoue Shrine” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system#Other shrines|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The annual festival is held on April 15. === Overview === Awayamanoue Shrine is located in the {{ill|lt=Katsura Village (Ibaraki Prefecture)|Q6378175|qid=Q6378175|lt=Katsura area|1=Katsura, Ibaraki}} in the northern part of Shirosato Town. {{ill|lt=National Route 123|Q6156750|qid=Q6156750|1=Japan National Route 123}} runs along the western edge of the shrine grounds. Despite the shrine name containing “mountain top” ({{ill|lt=Sanjo|Q11465835|qid=Q11465835|1=Sanjo}}), the surrounding area is flat land sandwiched between the Naka River and the {{ill|lt=Katsura River (Naka River system)|UNKNOWN|lt=Katsura River}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|qid=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}*, there is a tradition recorded as follows: “According to the sayings of the elders, in ancient times Oyama Village was called Kami-Awayama (Upper Awayama), and Awa-no Village was called Shimo-Awayama (Lower Awayama). The shrine was located above the two villages; therefore, it came to be called Awayamanoue.” Based on this tradition, the shrine name originally did not include the meaning of “on the mountain.” “Awa” (あわ) is an ancient place name found not only in the Engishiki entry for Awayamanoue Shrine but also in the Awa District of {{ill|lt=Naka District|Q1062968|qid=Q1062968|1=Naka District, Ibaraki}} recorded in the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|1=UNKNOWN}}*. In the Naka District section of the *{{ill|lt=Hitachi Fudoki|Q11481687|qid=Q11481687|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki}}*, the passage “To the northeast of the district, an official station was established across the '''Awagawa''' (粟河)” appears; this “Awagawa” is another name for the Naka River and is said to derive from its passage through Awa District. Awa District is thought to have comprised the area of present-day “Awayama” together with the neighboring southern area of “Awa” (formerly {{ill|lt=Katsura Village (Ibaraki Prefecture)|Q6378175|qid=Q6378175|lt=Awa Village|1=Katsura, Ibaraki}}). The *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* states, “This is said to be the place where millet originated in Hitachi Province,” preserving a tradition that this area was the birthplace of millet cultivation in Hitachi. The *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* also suggests that, because this area was well suited for millet cultivation, it was originally called “Awa” (written with the character for millet), but under the influence of the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō province system|Q860290|qid=Q860290|lt=Kōjirei (edict on auspicious characters)|1=Provinces of Japan}}, the characters “阿波” were adopted instead. Medieval and later documents still contain place names using the character for millet, such as Awa District, Kami-Awa (Upper Awa), and Shimo-Awa (Lower Awa). In {{ill|lt=Shōhei (Japan)|Q1143587|qid=Q1143587|lt=Shōhei|1=Shōhei}} 10 (1355), {{ill|lt=Satake Yoshiatsu|Q11383463|qid=Q11383463|1=Satake Yoshiatsu}} granted this area to Satake Yoshitaka, who took the name Ōyama and made Ōyama Castle on an island-like hill his residence; “Ōyama” is said to be a phonetic corruption of “Awayama.” In addition, Yoshinari, a younger brother of {{ill|lt=Satake Yoshimori|Q11383456|qid=Q11383456|1=Satake Yoshimori}} who lived in this area, styled himself “Awa Gyōbu no Daibu” and was known as “Awa-dono.” The name “Ōyama” remained the old village name until the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} (the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} era), when it was renamed {{ill|lt=Sawasan Village|Q11553149|qid=Q11553149|lt=Awayama Village|1=Sawayama}}. [[File:Awayamanoueno Shrine (Shirosato, Ibaraki, Japan) 01.jpg|thumb|The first torii gate. National Route 123 runs on the left side.]] === Enshrined deity === ; {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * From a tradition in which a child-shaped deity descended upon a great cedar tree holding {{ill|lt=Awa|Q161211|qid=Q161211|lt=ears of millet|1=Foxtail millet}} in his hand, the shrine is also known as “'''Furuki Myōjin'''” (also called “'''Sakariko Myōjin'''”). The *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* records that places such as Mount Furuki and Furuki Plain lay to the southwest of the main hall, indicating the existence of place names derived from this legend. * The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is a wooden standing statue in the form of a child. The *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* states, “The shintai is a wooden standing figure wearing {{ill|lt=sokutai|Q1156828|qid=Q1156828|1=Sokutai}},” while the *{{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryo|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}* records, “The sacred body is seated in the form of a child.” * Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto is a deity associated with millet, as according to the *{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}*, after completing the creation of the land, he was struck by a millet stalk (awagara) at {{ill|lt=Awashima|Q11559761|qid=Q11559761|1=Awashima}} and crossed over to the Eternal Land. * The {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|qid=Q48743901|1=Shinboku}} is a {{ill|lt=Japanese cedar|Q147388|qid=Q147388|lt=cedar|1=Cryptomeria}}. According to the shrine grounds’ “Origin of the Sacred Tree,” a gigantic cedar said to be over a thousand years old and revered as “Ōsugi-sama” once stood here as the second-generation sacred tree. However, it was struck by lightning on September 18, {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47 (1972), caught fire, and posed a danger of collapse, leading to its felling (approved by the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} on July 5, Shōwa 48). The current sacred tree was grown from a sapling of “Ōsugi-sama.” === Subsidiary shrines === There are eight subsidiary shrines: Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}), Fuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}), Hōsō Shrine ({{ill|qid=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|qid=Q595520|qid=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}), Tenman Shrine ({{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|qid=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}), Tennō Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), Ise Shinmei Shrine, Inari Shrine ({{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Ukanomitama}}), and Tōshō Tenman Shrine. Some sources do not list the Ise Shinmei Shrine or the Tōshō Tenman Shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|qid=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|lt=701|Q23496|qid=Q23496|1=701}}). In volume 49 of the *{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}*, under the entry for the 9th day of the 12th month of Ninwa 2 (886), it is recorded: “Granted Jūgoi-no-ge to Jūgoi-no-jō to the '''Awa Deity''' of Hitachi Province,” indicating that the “'''Awa Deity'''” was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is listed as the minor shrine “'''Awayamanoue Shrine'''” among the seven shrines (two major and five minor) of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|qid=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}} {{ill|lt=Naka District|Q1156492|qid=Q1156492|1=Naka District, Tokushima}}. In the former Katsura Village, there is also the minor shrine “Ishifune Shrine” of Naka District, Hitachi Province. In Kennin 4 (1216), {{ill|lt=Shinran|Q512934|qid=Q512934|1=Shinran}} is said to have relied on Amida-ji Temple located here to establish the Ōyama hermitage and to make it a base for missionary activities in the Okunaka District of Hitachi (origin of the Ōyama hermitage). At the entrance to the approach road stands a stone monument marking the site of the Ōyama hermitage, along with an explanatory signboard, and an Enmei Jizō is enshrined there. In Entoku 2 (1490), there is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Satake Yoshikiyo|Q11383467|qid=Q11383467|1=Satake Yoshikiyo}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|Q5369726|qid=Q5369726|lt=Ōta Castle|1=Ōda Castle}}, prayed at this shrine for victory in battle when he was driven out of Magone Castle by treacherous retainers. Feeling the divine favor, Yoshikiyo is said to have followed the custom of the shrine’s parishioners of not displaying kadomatsu decorations at New Year (from *Jōhoku Kyōdo Tokuhon*). From the medieval to early modern periods, the shrine was revered by successive local lords. * In the 11th month of Tenbun 10 (1541), Ōyama Yoshizumi rebuilt the shrine buildings (as recorded on a ridge tag). * In Tenshō 10 (1592), Ōyama Yoshikatsu carried out repairs (ridge tag). * During the Edo period, {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Nariaki|Q1323770|qid=Q1323770|1=Tokugawa Nariaki}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Mito Castle|Q848997|qid=Q848997|1=Mito Castle}}, donated 15 koku of shrine land and also repaired the shrine buildings. During the {{ill|lt=Tengu Party Rebellion|Q1040831|qid=Q1040831|1=Mito Rebellion}}, fighting took place within the shrine grounds. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was ranked as a gōsha. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2|refs= Nakamura (1981), pp. 30–31. In the shrine registry of the Ibaraki Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines, the reading “abasanjōjinja” is given; however, the place name Awayama is correctly read “awayama” (see Overview). The *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|qid=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}* gives the reading “Ahayamanouhe,” the *Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryo* gives “Ahayamanohenō,” and the *Jōhoku Kyōdo Tokuhon* gives “ahayama no he no.” “Yama no e” is considered a variant of “yama no ue.” *Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho*, entries for Awa District and Awayamanoue Shrine. *Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryo*, entry for Awayamanoue Shrine. In the *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*, the shrine section for “Awayamanoue Shrine” records the tradition that is the origin of “Furuki,” while the local section for “Awa District” contains documentary sources and analysis regarding place names. In addition, the village section for “Ōyama” includes descriptions of the shrine site, buildings, and shintai under the name “Sakariko Myōjin.” *Hyōchū Kofudoki*, Naka District section. Shirosato Town Chamber of Commerce official website, “Tourist Information (former Katsura area): Ōyama Castle Ruins and Ōyama Temple.” Accessed October 1, 2016. Currently, the Ōyama Castle site hosts an accommodation facility with a castle-style keep (Hotel Ōyama Castle). }} === References === * Nakamura Hiroshi, “Awayamanoue Shrine,” *Ibaraki Prefecture Encyclopedia*, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Shimbun|Q11617316|qid=Q11617316|lt=Ibaraki Shimbunsha|1=Ibaraki Shimbun}}, 1981. * Nakayama Nobuna, edited by {{ill|lt=Kurita Hiroshi|Q6536170|qid=Q6536170|1=Kurita Hiroshi}}, *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi, Volume 2*. Sekizenkai. Meiji 32–34 (1899–1901). * {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|qid=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}}, *Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho*, Vol. 2, 2nd ed. Fuzanbō. October 17, Meiji 40 (1907). * Meiji Jinja Shiryo Compilation Office (ed.), *Meiji Jinja Shiryo: Fuken Gōsha (Upper)*. Meiji Jinja Shiryo Compilation Office. Meiji 45 (1912). * Suzuka Tsunetane (comp.), *Jinja Kakuroku, Lower Volume*. Kōten Kenkyūjo. Meiji 35 (1902). * Northern Higashiibaraki District Education Association (ed.), *Jōhoku Kyōdo Tokuhon*. Northern Higashiibaraki District Education Association. Shōwa 9. * [http://www.shirosato.biz/ Shirosato Town Chamber of Commerce official website]. {{Shinto sidebar}} {{List of Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Awayamanoueno Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Awayamanoueno Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:城里町の建築物]] [[Category:Municipal History of Shirosato]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]]
